text: Elemental tellurium in molten $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2 - ZrF_4}$ is more difficult to reduce than $\mathrm{Ni}^{2 + }$ or $\mathrm{Fe}^{2 + }$ , because it is comparable to $\mathrm{Cr}^{2 + }$ and more easily reduced than $\mathrm{U}^{4 + }$ .
Bismuth, because of its use in fuel processing systems, $^{16}$ is a possible impurity in MSBR fuel salt.
For our previous work on bismuth, $^{17}$ the melt was contained in graphite or copper cells.
Bismuth (added as $\mathrm{BiF}_3$ ) was slowly lost from the molten LiF- $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ - $\mathrm{ZrF}_4$ at $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The two main routes for the loss would appear to be the reduction of the $\mathrm{Bi}^{3+}$ by the container material or volatilization of the $\mathrm{BiF}_3$ .
To further investigate this problem, a cell containing $\mathrm{Bi}^{3+}$ was set up; the melt was contained in a glassy carbon cell.
Well defined voltammograms and chronopotentiograms were obtained in a melt of approximately 10 millimoles in $\mathrm{BiF}_3$ at gold, iridium, and glassy carbon electrodes.

section: C. R. Hyman
text: Previously, a capsule had been designed for the study of the effect of fission products on prospective MSR containment materials.[25] The development and construction of the experiment had reached the point that only a small amount of work had to be performed before insertion of the capsule into the Oak Ridge Research Reactor (ORR).

section: C. R. Hyman
text: Since revival of the irradiation program, the work has been in the reorganization and fabrication of TcGen-1, which is the first in a series of related tests.

section: 10.10.1 Design
text: Each of the three test specimens or fuel pins consists of a 1/2-in.-OD $\times$ 0.43-in.-ID tube of the particular test material with welded end caps to provide a 3.5-in.-long internal cavity.

section: 10.10.1 Design
text: Each pin is filled with $7.14~\mathrm{cm}^3$ (3-in. depth) of fuel salt leaving a 1/2-in. helium-filled gas plenum at the top.

section: 10.10.1 Design
text: The wetted surface area in each specimen is $27.1~\mathrm{cm}^2$ .

section: 10.10.1 Design
text: The fuel composition is the same for each pin [LiF-BeF $_2$ -ZrF $_4$ -UF $_4$ (63.1-29.3-5.1-2.5 mole %)].

section: 10.10.1 Design
text: The uranium is ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ rather than ${}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ since ${}^{233}\mathrm{U}$ produces more tellurium (the suspected cause of cracking of Hastelloy N in the MSRE).

section: 10.10.1 Design
text: The design power, $34.4~\mathrm{W/cm}^3$ of salt ( $1.2~\mathrm{kW/ft}$ of fueled length), is expected to produce $5\times 10^{16}$ atoms of stable tellurium per square centimeter of wetted surface in the planned 1100-hr irradiation.

section: 10.10.1 Design
text: The specimen (fuel pin wall) operating temperature is $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

section: 10.10.1 Design
text: The annulus between the primary and secondary containment vessels is argon-filled and is sized to provide the thermal resistance required to achieve the design specimen temperature of $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ during operation.

section: 10.10.1 Design
text: Each specimen is provided with a 1/16-in.-OD stainless steel-sheathed heater coiled around the ends (Fig.

section: 10.10.1 Design
text: The heater serves the dual purpose of minimizing axial temperature variations at the ends of the specimens by providing heat where there is no fuel, and of maintaining the salt above $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ at all times when the reactor is down.

section: 10.10.2 Calculated Operation and Test Conditions
text: The capsule is designed to operate at a linear power density of $1.2\mathrm{kW / ft}$ in the fueled regions of the specimens and with a fuel-to-metal interface temperature of $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
